---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{Regression-Based Norms}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## The Standard Approach: A Quick Recap

When we interpret a test score, we compare it to a **normative sample**.

\pause \vfill

The most common approaches are:

- **z-scores** (parametric): how many SDs away from the mean?
- **Non-parametric percentiles**: what proportion of the sample scores below this value?
- **Crawford & Howell modified _t_-test**: is this single case significantly different from the normative group?

\pause \vfill

\small
All these methods have one thing in common: they require a **single, well-defined normative group**.


## The Problem: Who Should Be in the Group?

In neuropsychological and clinical assessment, performance is known to vary with:

\pause

- **Age** (e.g., memory declines with age)
- **Education** (e.g., higher education often boosts test scores)
- **Sex** (e.g., some tasks show sex differences)

\pause \vfill

A straightforward solution is to create **separate normative groups** for each combination of these variables.

\pause \vfill
\small
_But how exactly should we form these groups?_


## The Arbitrary Binning Problem

To create separate groups, we need to **bin** continuous variables like age and education.

\pause \vfill

For example:

- Age: 18--30, 31--45, 46--60, 61--75, 76+
- Education: $\le$ 8 years, 9--13 years, $\ge$ 14 years

\pause \vfill

**The problem**: these boundaries are \textbf{arbitrary}. Why 30 and not 35? Why 13 years and not 12?

\pause \vfill
\small
Binning decisions can affect classification outcomes — a person at the edge of a bin may be classified very differently depending on which cut-off is chosen.


## A Compounding Problem: Many Groups, Little Data

As we add more variables, the number of groups grows quickly.

\pause \vfill

With 3 age groups × 3 education levels × 2 sex groups = \textbf{18 separate normative groups}.

\pause \vfill

In practice:

- Some cells will have **very few participants**
- Norms from small groups are **unreliable**
- Some combinations may be **completely absent** from the normative sample

\pause \vfill
\small
_We need a way to model the influence of these variables continuously and simultaneously._


## A Potential Solution: Regression-Based Norms

Instead of dividing participants into groups, we can **model the relationship** between demographic variables and test scores using regression.

\pause \vfill

The general idea:

$$\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1 \cdot \text{Age} + b_2 \cdot \text{Education} + b_3 \cdot \text{Sex} + \ldots$$

\pause \vfill

The model provides a **predicted score** for any combination of age, education, and sex — without artificial binning.

\pause \vfill
\small
This approach was systematically formalised by Fastenau (1998) and later developed by Van der Elst and colleagues (2006 and onwards).


## From Predicted Scores to Norms

Once the regression model is fitted, each participant's score can be expressed **relative to what was expected** given their demographics.

\pause \vfill

The **residual** is the difference between the observed and the predicted score:

$$e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$$

\pause \vfill

These residuals can then be used in different ways:

- Expressed as **z-scores** using the residual standard deviation: $z = e_i / \text{SD}_e$
- Converted to **percentiles** using the empirical distribution of residuals
- Used in adapted **Crawford-type tests** applied to the residuals


## What Can We Do with the Residual-Based Score?

The standardised residual tells us: _how unusual is this score, given who this person is?_

\pause \vfill

A **z-score** based on the residuals allows us to:

- Apply familiar cut-offs (e.g., $z < -1.5$ or $z < -2$)
- Identify individuals whose performance is unexpectedly low (or high) \emph{after accounting for demographics}

\pause \vfill
\small
This is sometimes called the **standardised regression-based (SRB)** approach (Temkin et al., 1999).


## Advantages of Regression-Based Norms

Regression-based norms offer several practical advantages:

\pause

- **No arbitrary binning**: age, education, etc. are treated as continuous
- **Simultaneous adjustment**: all demographic variables are modelled together
- **More efficient use of data**: the full sample is used, not subgroups
- **Flexibility**: non-linear terms (e.g., $\text{Age}^2$) or interactions can be included
- **Applicable to any person**: predictions can be generated for any demographic combination within the observed range

\pause \vfill
\small
These properties make regression-based norms particularly useful when normative data are limited or when the population is demographically heterogeneous.


## Limitations and Open Problems

Regression-based norms are a valuable tool, but they come with important caveats.

\pause \vfill

**The model must fit well.**
If the regression model is misspecified (e.g., missing a non-linear effect), the residuals will not reflect true individual differences — they will also absorb model error.

\pause \vfill

**Many models can be fitted.**
Different choices of predictors, transformations, and functional forms can lead to different norms for the same person. There is no universally agreed procedure.

\pause \vfill
\small
_Which model is the "right" one? This question often lacks a definitive answer._


## Limitations: Edge Cases and Classification Risk

A particularly important concern is **what happens at the boundaries** of the normative space.

\pause \vfill

Regression models **interpolate well** but can **extrapolate poorly**:

- Very old individuals
- Very low or very high education levels
- Rare combinations of variables

\pause \vfill

In these cases, predictions are based on fewer observations and carry **greater uncertainty** — but this uncertainty is often not reflected in the final norm.

\pause \vfill
\small
Classification at the edge (e.g., "borderline impaired" vs. "normal") can be particularly risky for demographically extreme individuals.


## Summary

\begin{center}
\textbf{\Large Key Takeaways}
\end{center}
\vspace{1.5em}

\small

| Approach | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| z-score / percentile | Simple, transparent | Requires pre-defined group |
| Crawford _t_-test | Single-case inference | Requires pre-defined group |
| Regression-based | Continuous adjustment, efficient | Model-dependent, edge uncertainty |

\pause \vfill

\normalsize
Regression-based norms are a **principled and flexible** alternative to traditional normative approaches — but their validity depends heavily on the quality of the underlying model.

\pause \vfill
\small
\centering
_No method is assumption-free. Knowing the assumptions is the first step to using any method responsibly._

## Regression-based norms, percentiles and probabilities

As for Crawford t-tests there are also regression-based methods that use only percentiles and other regression methods that allow to make inferences at _population level_ (e.g. Crawford & Garthwaite 2006, Capitani & Laiacona, 1987).
